1: Yitrô kōhēn Midyān ḥōtēn Mōšeh: 'Jethro the priest of Midian, Moses's father-in-law.' Jethro is introduced by his religious title (kōhēn = priest) and his familial relationship. As kōhēn of Midian, he is a non-Israelite religious official whose knowledge of YHWH (Exodus 18:11) comes from prior revelation and now from hearing the news of the Exodus. The Gentile priest is the surprising vehicle of governance wisdom in this chapter.
2: ʾaḥar šillûḥêhā: 'after her sending away/dismissal.' This parenthetical note indicates Moses had sent Zipporah back to Jethro at some point. Šillûḥîm (dismissal, sending away) uses the same root as divorce terminology (Deuteronomy 24:1, 3), though here it simply means he sent her home. Jethro now returns her to Moses, restoring the family unit before the Sinai encounter.
3: Gēršōm: from gēr šām (a sojourner there) — Moses's naming theology embedded in his son's name. The gēr (resident alien/sojourner) is the central social category in the Exodus narrative: the law will repeatedly ground concern for the sojourner in Israel's own sojourn experience (Exodus 22:21; Exodus 23:9; Leviticus 19:33-34). The name carries the Exodus's social ethic.
4: ʾĔlîʿezer: 'my God is help' — ʾĔlî (my God) + ʿezer (help), the same root as in ʿezer kĕnegdô (Genesis 2:18). The specific rescue cited: wayyaṣṣilēnî mēḥereb parʿōh ('he delivered me from Pharaoh's sword'). The hiphil of nāṣal (to rescue/deliver) is key Exodus vocabulary for YHWH's saving action. The name commemorates personal deliverance that precedes and anticipates the national deliverance.
5: ʾel-hāhār ʾĕšer hûʾ ḥōneh šām: 'to the mountain where he was encamped.' The burning bush mountain (Horeb/Sinai, Exodus 3:1) is the destination toward which the whole wilderness journey has been oriented. Jethro comes to meet Moses at the mountain of God — the site of prior revelation is the site of this reunion. The movement brings the Gentile priest to the sacred center of YHWH's revelation to Israel.
6: Jethro sends word ahead of his coming — the proper protocol for approaching a leader. ʾĂnî ḥōtĕnĕkā Yitrô bāʾ ʾēlêkā: 'I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you.' The self-identification with relationship category (ḥōtēn = father-in-law) signals the domestic register of the visit — family reunion before political consultation.
7: wayyēṣēʾ mōšeh liqrāʾt ḥōtĕnô wayyištaḥû wayyiššaq lô: 'Moses went out to meet his father-in-law, bowed down, and kissed him.' Three verbs capture the full protocol of respectful greeting: going out to meet (liqrāʾt = a formal reception), prostrating (šāḥāh, the same verb used of worship), and kissing (nāšaq). The greeting is the full covenant-community hospitality gesture, parallel to Abraham meeting the three visitors (Genesis 18:2).
8: wayyĕsapper Mōšeh lĕḥōtĕnô ʾēt kol-ʾăšer ʿāśāh YHWH: 'Moses told his father-in-law all that YHWH had done.' The verb sāpar (to count/recount/tell) is the same root as sēper (book/document) — telling is a form of inscription. The narrative covers: YHWH's acts against Pharaoh and Egypt; all the hardship (tĕlāʾāh) along the way; and how YHWH had delivered them. Moses's recounting is an act of covenant witness — he testifies to the Gentile priest.
9: wayyiḥad Yitrô: 'Jethro rejoiced.' The verb ḥādāh (to rejoice/be glad) is relatively rare; it appears in the Psalms and here. Jethro's joy is specified: ʿal kol-haṭṭôbāh (over all the good) that YHWH had done for Israel. The affective response to the news of redemption is joy — the appropriate and full response to the gospel of deliverance.
10: Bārûk YHWH ʾăšer hiṣṣîl ʾetkem: 'Blessed be YHWH who delivered you.' Jethro's benediction is a berakah formula — the standard blessing-formula that acknowledges YHWH as the source of good. The double miyyad (from the hand of) emphasizes comprehensive rescue: from Egyptian power as a system and from Pharaoh's personal power. The Gentile priest speaks the covenant community's liturgy of gratitude.
11: ʿattāh yādaʿtî kî gādôl YHWH mikkol-hāʾĕlōhîm: 'Now I know that YHWH is greater than all the gods.' Jethro's confession is the theological climax of the chapter. ʿAttāh yādaʿtî (now I know) — the experiential knowledge formula, knowledge through hearing testimony. The comparative gādôl mikkol-hāʾĕlōhîm (greater than all the gods) asserts YHWH's superiority. Kî baddābār ʾăšer zādû ʿălêhem ('for in the thing in which they acted arrogantly') specifies the basis: Egypt's gods sponsored Egyptian arrogance, and YHWH defeated them at every point. The Gentile priest confesses what Israel often forgets.
12: wayyiqqaḥ Yitrô ḥōtēn Mōšeh ʿōlāh ûzĕbāḥîm lēʾlōhîm: 'Jethro brought a burnt offering and sacrifices to God.' ʿÔlāh (burnt offering — wholly consumed, ascending to God) and zĕbāḥîm (peace/fellowship offerings — shared between God and worshippers) together constitute a complete worship act. Aaron and all the elders of Israel join the meal — Jethro's sacrifices are the occasion for a covenant meal that includes the leadership of Israel. The Gentile priest participates in Israel's liturgy.
13: wayyēšeb Mōšeh lišpōṭ ʾet-hāʿām ... min-habbōqer ʿad-hāʿāreb: 'Moses sat to judge the people ... from morning until evening.' The verb šāpaṭ (to judge, decide, govern) covers arbitration, rule-declaration, and case adjudication. The phrase min-habbōqer ʿad-hāʿāreb (from morning until evening) captures the unsustainability of one-person governance — Moses as sole judge creates a system that consumes its most gifted member.
14: wayyarʾ ḥōtēn Mōšeh ʾēt kol-ʾăšer hûʾ ʿōśeh lāʿām: 'Moses's father-in-law saw everything he was doing for the people.' Jethro's observation is the outsider's diagnostic perspective. Maddûaʿ ʾattāh yōšēb lĕbaddekā ('why do you sit alone?') — baddekā means 'by yourself alone.' The problem is systemic, not a matter of Moses's dedication; the system's architecture is defective.
15: kî-yābōʾ ʾēlay hāʿām lidrōš ʾĕlōhîm: 'because the people come to me to inquire of God.' The verb dāraš (to seek/inquire) is the technical term for consulting an oracle or judicial authority. Lidrōš ʾĕlōhîm (to inquire of God) — Moses's role is not merely legal arbiter but mediator of divine decision. Every case is framed as a consultation with God, which explains both the burden and the bottleneck.
16: kî yihyeh lāhem dābār bāʾ ʾēlay ûšāpaṭtî bên ʾîš ûbên rēʿēhû: 'when they have a dispute they come to me, and I judge between a man and his neighbor.' Moses also teaches the decrees and laws of God — the judge is simultaneously teacher and case-arbiter. The dual function (teaching and adjudicating) is the foundation of the Levitical-priestly and later rabbinic judicial role.
17: lōʾ-ṭôb haddābār ʾăšer ʾattāh ʿōśeh: 'the thing you are doing is not good.' Jethro's assessment uses the emphatic construction: nābōl tibbōl gam-ʾattāh gam-hāʿām hazzeh — 'you will surely wear out, both you and this people.' Nābōl tibbōl is the Qal infinitive absolute + imperfect: 'wearing out you will wear out.' The verb nābal (to wither/wilt/wear out) connects physical exhaustion with a kind of organizational foolishness.
18: nābōl tibbōl: the emphatic infinitive absolute confirms inevitability. Kî kābēd mimmĕkā haddābār ('the thing is too heavy for you') — kābēd (heavy) recurs from Exodus 17:12 (Moses's heavy hands). The same heaviness that required Aaron and Hur's support in battle now requires delegation in governance. Physical and organizational weight are parallel problems with parallel solutions: delegation and support structures.
19: ʾĕhyeh ʿimmāk lēʾlōhîm: 'be to God for the people.' Moses's role is defined as the people's representative before God. The phrase uses ʾĕhyeh (the I AM name) in a human application — Moses functions as the mediator of the I AM's presence. He stands between people and God: bringing their cases upward, bringing God's statutes and decisions downward. This mediatorial role is the OT pattern for the high priest and ultimately for Christ (Hebrews 9:24).
20: wĕhizahartāh ʾethem ʾet-haḥuqqîm wĕʾet-hattôrōt wĕhôdaʿtā lāhem ʾet-hadderek: Four vocabulary items: ḥuqqîm (statutes — fixed, often cultic decrees), tôrôt (plural of tôrāh — instructions, from yārāh to shoot/direct), hadderek (the way — the halakic pattern), and hammaʿăśeh (the work/deeds). The judicial role is inseparable from the teaching role: judges must first be teachers of the law they administer. Yārāh (the root of tôrāh) means to shoot/aim/direct — Torah is divine direction for the path of life.
21: ʾanšê ḥayil yirĕʾê ʾĕlōhîm ʾanšê ʾĕmet śōnĕʾê beṣaʿ: four qualifications for judges — 'capable men, God-fearing, men of truth, haters of unjust gain.' Ḥayil (capable/competent — the word for virtue in Ruth 3:11 and Proverbs 31:10) covers intellectual and practical competence. Yirĕʾê ʾĕlōhîm (God-fearing) is the foundational orientation — judicial wisdom begins with the fear of YHWH (Proverbs 1:7). ʾĔmet (truth/faithfulness — both cognitive and moral accuracy). Śōnĕʾê beṣaʿ (haters of dishonest gain/bribery) — incorruptibility. This fourfold profile becomes the standard for Israelite leadership (Deuteronomy 1:13-17; Exodus 16:18-20).
22: kol-haddābār haggādōl yabiʾû ʾēlêkā wĕkol-haddābār haqqāṭōn yišpĕṭû hēm: 'every great matter they shall bring to you, but every small matter they shall judge themselves.' The graduated judicial hierarchy: minor cases decided locally; significant cases escalated to Moses. Gādôl and qāṭōn become the standing terms for case-scale in Israelite jurisprudence (Deuteronomy 1:17). This architecture anticipates the Sanhedrin's three-tiered system (courts of 3, 23, and 71).
23: wĕṣiwwĕkā ʾĕlōhîm wĕyākaltā ʿămōd: 'and God so commands you, you will be able to stand.' Jethro conditions the entire plan on divine sanction — the reform is not his imposition but must be validated by covenant authority. Moses does not implement immediately; the chapter ends with Moses doing as Jethro said, but the assumption is that YHWH's approval is sought. Governance wisdom is subsumed under covenant authority.
24: wayyišmaʿ Mōšeh lĕqôl ḥōtĕnô wayyaʿas kōl ʾăšer ʾāmar: 'Moses listened to his father-in-law's voice and did everything he said.' The verb šāmaʿ (to hear/obey) carries both meanings. Moses's willingness to be instructed by a Gentile priest — taking counsel from outside the covenant community — demonstrates the humility described in Numbers 12:3 ('Moses was very meek'). Wisdom is not exclusive to the covenant community; YHWH's common grace distributes organizational insight broadly.
25: šārê ʾălāpîm šārê mēʾôt šārê ḥămiššîm wĕšārê ʿăśārōt: 'commanders of thousands, commanders of hundreds, commanders of fifties, commanders of tens.' The decimal hierarchy creates a scalable governance structure. The word śar (commander/prince/leader) is used for both military commanders and civil leaders. The decimal organization mirrors the census structure of Num 1 and the military organization of Deuteronomy. Governance and military command share the same hierarchical architecture.
26: ʾEt-haddābār haqqāšeh (the hard/difficult matter) — qāšeh (hard, stiff, difficult) is used of hard labor in Egypt (Exodus 1:14) and the hardened heart of Pharaoh (Exodus 7:3). The difficult cases require concentrated attention that the Exodus itself required. The gradation of difficulty structures legal access to Moses: only the genuinely complex reaches the covenant mediator.
27: wayyĕšallaḥ Mōšeh ʾet-ḥōtĕnô wayyēlek lô ʾel-ʾarṣô: 'Moses sent his father-in-law away, and he went to his own land.' Jethro departs before Sinai — he does not receive the covenant directly (though Numbers 10:29-32 suggests he or his son Hobab may have traveled with Israel). The Gentile priest who confessed YHWH's supremacy, offered sacrifice, participated in the covenant meal, and reformed Israel's judicial system returns home. His contribution is complete; the Sinai narrative awaits.
